stem: Let \( p = [[p]] \), a prime, and let \( a = [[a]] \) (with \( p \nmid a \)). Using Fermat's Little Theorem, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \) is divided by \( [[p]] \).
solution: By Fermat's Little Theorem, \( a^{p-1}\equiv 1\pmod p \), so reduce the exponent modulo \( p-1=[[p]]-1 \) before computing the power: \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[p]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly reduces the exponent modulo \( p \) instead of modulo \( p-1 \).
This shifts the exponent down by one instead of correctly reducing it modulo \( p-1 \).
This reduces \( a \) itself modulo \( p-1 \), instead of reducing the EXPONENT modulo \( p-1 \).
This swaps the roles of the base and the exponent.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.

stem: Let \( n = [[n]] \) and \( a = [[a]] \), with \( \gcd(a,n)=1 \). Using Euler's Theorem, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \) is divided by \( [[n]] \).
solution: By Euler's Theorem, \( a^{\varphi(n)}\equiv 1\pmod n \) since \( \gcd(a,n)=1 \); here \( \varphi([[n]]) = [[phi]] \), so reduce the exponent modulo \( [[phi]] \) before computing the power: \( [[a]]^{[[k]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[n]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly reduces the exponent modulo \( n \) instead of modulo \( \varphi(n) \).
This shifts the exponent down by one instead of correctly reducing it modulo \( \varphi(n) \).
This reduces \( a \) itself modulo \( \varphi(n) \), instead of reducing the EXPONENT modulo \( \varphi(n) \).
This swaps the roles of the base and the exponent.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.

stem: Let \( p = [[p]] \), a prime. Using Fermat's Little Theorem, find the modular inverse of \( [[a]] \) modulo \( [[p]] \) — that is, the integer \( x \) with \( 0 \le x < [[p]] \) such that \( [[a]]\,x \equiv 1 \pmod{[[p]]} \).
solution: By Fermat's Little Theorem, \( a^{p-1}\equiv 1\pmod p \), so \( a^{p-2} \) is the modular inverse of \( a \): \( [[a]]^{[[p]]-2} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[p]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the exponent \( p-1 \), which always gives 1, not the inverse.
This just reports \( a \) itself reduced mod \( p \), not its inverse.
This uses one power too few, \( p-3 \) instead of \( p-2 \).
This subtracts the correct inverse from \( p \), flipping to the wrong residue.
This is one more than the correct inverse.
This is one less than the correct inverse.
